target.title: Add hosted external catalog feed loader
target.body: <!-- hosted-feed-stack:start -->## Hosted Marketplace Feed StackThis is PR2 in the hosted marketplace feed stack. The stack should land in order, with maintainer review at each step before the later PRs wire hosted marketplace data into broader user flows.1. #95846: refactor the bundled external plugin catalog toward the feed-shaped data model.2. #95868: add the lazy hosted marketplace feed loader for the configured public feed URL, with guarded HTTPS fetch, checksum, schema, size, timeout, and bundled fallback behavior.3. #95877: add accepted hosted snapshot fallback so a failed or unchanged hosted fetch can reuse the last valid feed payload.4. #95964: persist hosted marketplace feed snapshots in OpenClaw state.5. #95969: add marketplace source profile validation for hosted feed install candidates.6. #95981: add configurable `marketplaces.feeds` and `marketplaces.sources` profiles.7. #96155: add `openclaw plugins marketplace refresh` as the explicit manual snapshot refresh path.8. #96158: add `openclaw plugins marketplace entries` as the explicit manual read path over hosted, snapshot, or bundled fallback entries.9. #96194: add marketplace feed telemetry.The broader follow-up after this stack is to decide whether and how marketplace entries should feed install/search/startup behavior, add automatic conditional refresh using `ETag` / `Last-Modified`, or move to ClawHub producer-side validation for `/v1/feeds/plugins` and `/v1/feeds/skills`.<!-- hosted-feed-stack:end -->## Journey ContextThis is step 2 of the hosted OpenClaw feed path from RFC 19. #95846 made the bundled catalog feed-shaped. This PR adds the guarded hosted fetch path for that same marketplace feed, while leaving the existing synchronous bundled-catalog startup path unchanged.## What This PR Does- Adds an async hosted loader for the configured public marketplace feed URL.- Routes hosted fetches through the existing SSRF guard with HTTPS required and the allowed public feed host constrained.- Sends optional `If-None-Match` and `If-Modified-Since` headers so callers can perform freshness checks.- Reads response bodies with byte and chunk-timeout limits.- Keeps the network guard import lazy so bundled-catalog cold paths do not load the guarded network stack.- Computes `sha256` metadata for the hosted response body.- Validates the hosted document against the feed-shaped official external plugin catalog schema.- Falls back to the bundled catalog on network, status, checksum, size, JSON, or schema failures.`n- Leaves hosted candidate-to-install metadata synthesis out of this transport slice; #95969 owns source-profile validation and ClawHub candidate integrity semantics.## What This PR Intentionally Does Not DoThis PR does not wire the hosted loader into startup, add persistent snapshots, add source-profile selection, interpret `install.candidates[].integrity`, add auth, add signed envelopes, add regional feeds, or change plugin install/search behavior. #95877 adds the loader-level last-known-good snapshot contract on top of this fetch path, and #95969 owns source-profile validation plus ClawHub checksum semantics.## How To ReviewReview this as the hosted transport and validation layer. The important contract is: a hosted marketplace feed can be tried safely, but any unsafe or unusable hosted result falls back to the bundled catalog instead of changing current behavior.## Validation- `pnpm exec oxfmt src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`- `pnpm exec oxlint --deny-warnings src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`- `git diff --check`- `CI=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts` (25 tests)- `timeout 300s pnpm tsgo:core:test`## Real Behavior ProofBehavior or issue addressed:The hosted catalog fetch path uses the live ClawHub plugin feed by default, accepts the live schema v2 wrapper, preserves raw marketplace package metadata without synthesizing OpenClaw install metadata, and still falls back safely on invalid hosted responses.Real environment tested:Windows checkout `C:\tmp\openclaw-feeds-proof\pr2` at pushed head `2d68751c140f`, source harness via `node --import tsx`, live HTTPS request to `https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins`.Exact steps or command run after this patch:1. `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`2. `.\node_modules\.bin\oxfmt.cmd --check src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`3. `.\node_modules\.bin\oxlint.cmd --deny-warnings src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`4. `git diff --check`5. `node --import tsx -e "... loadHostedOfficialExternalPluginCatalogEntries({ snapshotStore: null }) ..."`Evidence after fix:```json{  "head": "ac2cbe0e94",  "defaultFeedUrl": "https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins",  "resultSource": "hosted",  "metadata": { "url": "https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins", "status": 200, "checksum": "sha256:90a122c19017ef2c7a0607c8c1cb893ae7871537fe649d39080a52547bed0a7f" },  "entryCount": 59,  "feed": { "id": "clawhub-official", "sequence": 11 },  "firstEntries": [    { "name": "@expediagroup/expedia-openclaw", "id": "@expediagroup/expedia-openclaw", "label": "Expedia Travel", "npmSpec": "@expediagroup/expedia-openclaw@1.0.4" }  ]}```Observed result after fix:The default hosted fetch accepted the live ClawHub feed as `hosted`, returned hosted entries, preserved response checksum/validators, and left marketplace candidates as raw feed metadata so #95969 owns source-profile install interpretation.What was not tested:Snapshot reuse and candidate-to-install interpretation are not part of this PR. Snapshot reuse is covered by #95877; source-profile install interpretation is covered by #95969.

target.title: fix(agents): bound model catalog JSON response reads for bedrock-mantle and huggingface
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Amazon Bedrock Mantle model discovery (`/v1/models`) and HuggingFace model
discovery (`/models`) both parse successful HTTP responses with an unbounded
`await response.json()`. On Node, `response.json()` buffers the entire body
before parsing, so a provider-controlled endpoint can stream an arbitrarily
large or never-ending JSON payload and push the discovery path into memory
pressure, hangs, or OOM.

That matters here because both inputs are untrusted:

- Mantle discovery reads from an operator-selected AWS endpoint.
- HuggingFace discovery reads from an external router endpoint.

This PR closes the same response-body gap already fixed for sibling catalog
surfaces in `#95418`, `#95420`, and `#96027`, but for the two remaining bundled
provider discovery reads still using unbounded success-path JSON parsing on
current `main`.

## Changes

- Replace the Mantle success-path read in `discoverMantleModels` with
  `readProviderJsonResponse(response, "bedrock-mantle.model-discovery")`.
- Replace the HuggingFace success-path read in
  `discoverHuggingfaceModels` with
  `readProviderJsonResponse(response, "huggingface.model-discovery")`.
- Reuse the existing shared provider JSON reader instead of introducing a new
  provider-local helper. That helper already enforces the standard 16 MiB JSON
  byte cap and wraps malformed JSON with a caller label.
- Preserve the existing fail-soft behavior around the changed call sites:
  Mantle still falls back to cached models or `[]` on discovery failure, and
  HuggingFace still falls back to the static bundled catalog on discovery
  failure.
- Update Mantle discovery tests to return real `Response` objects instead of
  plain `{ json() {} }` mocks so the changed path uses the same body/stream API
  shape as production.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** a successful catalog response with no
  `Content-Length` must not be buffered whole if it exceeds the JSON safety cap;
  the read must stop near the cap, cancel the stream, and raise a bounded
  overflow error instead of draining the full body.
- **Real environment tested:** a real local `node:http` server bound to
  `127.0.0.1`, streaming a JSON body in 64 KiB chunks with **no**
  `Content-Length`, driven through the real exported bounded reader
  `readResponseWithLimit` that `readProviderJsonResponse` uses internally.
- **Exact proof shape:**
  1. Stream an oversized JSON body (~24 MiB) from `/huge`.
  2. Drive `readResponseWithLimit(response, 1 MiB, { onOverflow })` against the
     stream and assert bounded rejection plus early socket close.
  3. Run the negative control `await response.json()` against the same `/huge`
     stream and measure full-body drain.
  4. Drive the same bounded helper against `/small` and confirm a normal
     catalog-shaped JSON body still parses intact.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - Bounded case: threw
    `bedrock-mantle.model-discovery: JSON response exceeds 1048576 bytes`.
  - Early cancel: the server socket closed after only **1,064,178 bytes**
    reached the wire, near the 1 MiB cap rather than the full ~24 MiB body.
  - Negative control: the old unbounded `response.json()` path pulled the full
    **25,165,824 bytes**, proving the cap is load-bearing.
  - Happy path: a small valid catalog body still parsed into exactly one model
    with its `id` intact.
- **Suite coverage:** the in-repo Vitest run passed **5/5 files, 48/48 tests**,
  including the updated Mantle discovery tests that now use real `Response`
  objects on the success path.
- **What this proof does and does not prove:** this demonstrates real bytes on
  wire, early stream cancellation, and unchanged small-body parsing through the
  exact bounded transport helper the new call sites use. It does **not** yet
  directly drive `discoverMantleModels` or `discoverHuggingfaceModels` against
  a hostile local streaming endpoint.

## Evidence

```text
[case 1] oversized catalog JSON, cap=1024 KiB, NO content-length
  ok: readResponseWithLimit rejected on oversized body — true
  ok: bounded error message present (got: bedrock-mantle.model-discovery: JSON response exceeds 1048576 bytes) — true
  ok: bytes on wire stayed near the cap, not the full body (sent 1064178) — true

[negative control] OLD unbounded `await response.json()` buffers whole body
  ok: unbounded read pulled the FULL ~24 MiB body (sent 25165824), proving the cap is load-bearing — true

[case 3] normal small catalog JSON still parses unchanged
  ok: small catalog body parsed into 1 model — true
  ok: model id intact (valid small body not truncated) — true

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

target.title: fix(agents): serialize structured non-image tool-result blocks in extractToolResultText (fixes #97267)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #97267.

`extractToolResultText` only collected explicit `text` content blocks from tool results. When a tool result contained only structured non-image blocks (JSON, resource, etc.), it returned `undefined` — causing the tool result to collapse to an attachment placeholder in the visible transcript.

## Changes Made

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.tools.ts`: Added a structured-block fallback inside `extractToolResultText`. When no text blocks are present, the fallback serializes structured non-image blocks via `JSON.stringify`. `text` and `image` blocks are skipped in the fallback: images remain handled by media delivery, and text blocks already returned directly.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.tools.test.ts`: Added 5 focused tests covering structured fallback, image skip, mixed text+structured separation, and edge cases.

## Evidence

- **Unit tests:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.tools.test.ts` — 56/56 passed
- **Lint:** `npx oxlint --tsconfig tsconfig.json src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.tools.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.tools.test.ts` — clean
- **Lockfile:** unchanged

## Test coverage

| Scenario | Status |
|----------|:---:|
| Non-object result → undefined | ✅ |
| Explicit text blocks → collected | ✅ |
| Structured non-image block → JSON-serialized | ✅ |
| Image-only block → undefined (media delivery) | ✅ |
| Mixed text + structured → text returned, fallback skipped | ✅ |

**What was not tested:** Live end-to-end tool subscription path (fix is in the shared `extractToolResultText` utility, unit-level verification suffices).

## AI Assistance 🤖

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
- **Human confirmed understanding**: Yes

target.title: fix(agents): bound model catalog JSON response reads for bedrock-mantle and huggingface
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Amazon Bedrock Mantle model discovery (`/v1/models`) and HuggingFace model
discovery (`/models`) both parse successful HTTP responses with an unbounded
`await response.json()`. On Node, `response.json()` buffers the entire body
before parsing, so a provider-controlled endpoint can stream an arbitrarily
large or never-ending JSON payload and push the discovery path into memory
pressure, hangs, or OOM.

That matters here because both inputs are untrusted:

- Mantle discovery reads from an operator-selected AWS endpoint.
- HuggingFace discovery reads from an external router endpoint.

This PR closes the same response-body gap already fixed for sibling catalog
surfaces in `#95418`, `#95420`, and `#96027`, but for the two remaining bundled
provider discovery reads still using unbounded success-path JSON parsing on
current `main`.

## Changes

- Replace the Mantle success-path read in `discoverMantleModels` with
  `readProviderJsonResponse(response, "bedrock-mantle.model-discovery")`.
- Replace the HuggingFace success-path read in
  `discoverHuggingfaceModels` with
  `readProviderJsonResponse(response, "huggingface.model-discovery")`.
- Reuse the existing shared provider JSON reader instead of introducing a new
  provider-local helper. That helper already enforces the standard 16 MiB JSON
  byte cap and wraps malformed JSON with a caller label.
- Preserve the existing fail-soft behavior around the changed call sites:
  Mantle still falls back to cached models or `[]` on discovery failure, and
  HuggingFace still falls back to the static bundled catalog on discovery
  failure.
- Update Mantle discovery tests to return real `Response` objects instead of
  plain `{ json() {} }` mocks so the changed path uses the same body/stream API
  shape as production.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** a successful catalog response with no
  `Content-Length` must not be buffered whole if it exceeds the JSON safety cap;
  the read must stop near the cap, cancel the stream, and raise a bounded
  overflow error instead of draining the full body.
- **Real environment tested:** a real local `node:http` server bound to
  `127.0.0.1`, streaming a JSON body in 64 KiB chunks with **no**
  `Content-Length`, driven through the real exported bounded reader
  `readResponseWithLimit` that `readProviderJsonResponse` uses internally.
- **Exact proof shape:**
  1. Stream an oversized JSON body (~24 MiB) from `/huge`.
  2. Drive `readResponseWithLimit(response, 1 MiB, { onOverflow })` against the
     stream and assert bounded rejection plus early socket close.
  3. Run the negative control `await response.json()` against the same `/huge`
     stream and measure full-body drain.
  4. Drive the same bounded helper against `/small` and confirm a normal
     catalog-shaped JSON body still parses intact.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - Bounded case: threw
    `bedrock-mantle.model-discovery: JSON response exceeds 1048576 bytes`.
  - Early cancel: the server socket closed after only **1,064,178 bytes**
    reached the wire, near the 1 MiB cap rather than the full ~24 MiB body.
  - Negative control: the old unbounded `response.json()` path pulled the full
    **25,165,824 bytes**, proving the cap is load-bearing.
  - Happy path: a small valid catalog body still parsed into exactly one model
    with its `id` intact.
- **Suite coverage:** the in-repo Vitest run passed **5/5 files, 48/48 tests**,
  including the updated Mantle discovery tests that now use real `Response`
  objects on the success path.
- **What this proof does and does not prove:** this demonstrates real bytes on
  wire, early stream cancellation, and unchanged small-body parsing through the
  exact bounded transport helper the new call sites use. It does **not** yet
  directly drive `discoverMantleModels` or `discoverHuggingfaceModels` against
  a hostile local streaming endpoint.

## Evidence

```text
[case 1] oversized catalog JSON, cap=1024 KiB, NO content-length
  ok: readResponseWithLimit rejected on oversized body — true
  ok: bounded error message present (got: bedrock-mantle.model-discovery: JSON response exceeds 1048576 bytes) — true
  ok: bytes on wire stayed near the cap, not the full body (sent 1064178) — true

[negative control] OLD unbounded `await response.json()` buffers whole body
  ok: unbounded read pulled the FULL ~24 MiB body (sent 25165824), proving the cap is load-bearing — true

[case 3] normal small catalog JSON still parses unchanged
  ok: small catalog body parsed into 1 model — true
  ok: model id intact (valid small body not truncated) — true

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

```text
Test Files  5 passed (5)
     Tests  48 passed (48)
  Duration  ~8s
```

**Label**: security

target.title: fix(launchd): verify profile updater jobs by metadata
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A macOS update handoff can leave behind a profile-scoped launchd label like `ai.openclaw.<profile>.update.<timestamp>`, which can keep rerunning `openclaw update --yes` after the gateway is already healthy.

PR #96924 was closed because a label-only matcher could also classify a legitimate gateway profile whose name contains `.update.<timestamp>`. This replacement keeps the bug fix but removes that false-positive path.

## Why This Change Was Made

Profile-scoped update labels are now treated as candidates only. Before stale-job reporting or disabling, OpenClaw confirms the launchd job through metadata:

- explicit `OPENCLAW_UPDATE_RUN_HANDOFF=1` in the LaunchAgent environment, or
- LaunchAgent `ProgramArguments` that look like an OpenClaw update command with `update --yes`, and not a gateway command.

Legacy `ai.openclaw.update.*` and `ai.openclaw.manual-update.<digits>` behavior remains unchanged. The scanner process environment marker is intentionally not trusted for other profile-scoped labels.

## User Impact

OpenClaw can detect and disable the stuck profile-scoped updater loop, while `status`/`doctor` will not recommend disabling gateway jobs whose profile names happen to look update-shaped.

## Evidence

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/daemon/launchd.ts src/daemon/launchd.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/launchd.test.ts` -> 94 passed
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/daemon/launchd.ts src/daemon/launchd.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `git diff --check`

### macOS launchd behavior proof

Ran from this branch on macOS with two temporary LaunchAgents under `gui/501`, both created with `RunAtLoad=false` and removed immediately after capture. The positive control used profile-scoped updater metadata; the negative control used a gateway command under a label that still matches `ai.openclaw.<profile>.update.<timestamp>`.

```text
macOS launchd profile-updater proof
domain=gui/501
temporary labels:
  updater=ai.openclaw.proofcody.update.20260627T160051Z
  gateway_control=ai.openclaw.dev.team.update.20260627T160051Z
launchctl list matching labels:
  -	0	ai.openclaw.dev.team.update.20260627T160051Z
  -	0	ai.openclaw.proofcody.update.20260627T160051Z
metadata excerpts:
  updater ProgramArguments=[/opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw, update, --yes, --json]
  updater EnvironmentVariables.OPENCLAW_UPDATE_RUN_HANDOFF=1
  gateway_control ProgramArguments=[/opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw, gateway, run]
OpenClaw findStaleOpenClawUpdateLaunchdJobs filtered output:
[
  {
    "label": "ai.openclaw.proofcody.update.20260627T160051Z",
    "lastExitStatus": 0
  }
]
expected: updater label reported; gateway_control label ignored
cleanup launchctl list remaining labels:
  none
```

target.title: Add hosted catalog source profile validation
target.body: <!-- hosted-feed-stack:start -->## Hosted Marketplace Feed StackThis is PR5 in the hosted marketplace feed stack. The stack should land in order, with maintainer review at each step before the later PRs wire hosted marketplace data into broader user flows.1. #95846: refactor the bundled external plugin catalog toward the feed-shaped data model.2. #95868: add the lazy hosted marketplace feed loader for the configured public feed URL, with guarded HTTPS fetch, checksum, schema, size, timeout, and bundled fallback behavior.3. #95877: add accepted hosted snapshot fallback so a failed or unchanged hosted fetch can reuse the last valid feed payload.4. #95964: persist hosted marketplace feed snapshots in OpenClaw state.5. #95969: add marketplace source profile validation for hosted feed install candidates.6. #95981: add configurable `marketplaces.feeds` and `marketplaces.sources` profiles.7. #96155: add `openclaw plugins marketplace refresh` as the explicit manual snapshot refresh path.8. #96158: add `openclaw plugins marketplace entries` as the explicit manual read path over hosted, snapshot, or bundled fallback entries.9. #96194: add marketplace feed telemetry.The broader follow-up after this stack is to decide whether and how marketplace entries should feed install/search/startup behavior, add automatic conditional refresh using `ETag` / `Last-Modified`, or move to ClawHub producer-side validation for `/v1/feeds/plugins` and `/v1/feeds/skills`.<!-- hosted-feed-stack:end -->## Stack ContextThis is step 5 in the hosted feed rollout, stacked on `feeds-hosted-catalog-persistent-snapshot` after:- #95846 reshapes the bundled external plugin catalog as a feed-shaped fallback.- #95868 adds the guarded hosted feed loader.- #95877 adds the last-known-good snapshot fallback contract.- #95964 persists hosted catalog snapshots in OpenClaw state.## RFC AlignmentThis PR adds the first local feed/source profile scaffolding from RFC #19. The important boundary is that profile names are local OpenClaw configuration. A hosted feed can refer to a configured `sourceRef`, but it cannot define registry domains, credentials, or trust roots for the client.This matches the live ClawHub plugin feed at `https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins`, which uses `public-clawhub` candidates. The PR also keeps the older register host allowed for transition, while `registry.openclaw.ai` is still being brought online.## Summary- Adds local profile types for hosted catalog feeds and package sources.- Adds the default `clawhub-public` feed profile pointing at `https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins`.- Adds the default `public-clawhub` source profile for ClawHub candidates and keeps `public-npm` for npm-backed candidates.- Lets named local feed profiles authorize their configured HTTPS host through the existing SSRF guard path.- Keeps direct ad hoc `feedUrl` overrides constrained to the public host allowlist, including `clawhub.ai` and the earlier register host.- Filters feed entries that opt into `install.candidates[].sourceRef` when the source ref is missing or not configured locally.- Applies the same source-ref filtering to hosted responses and hosted snapshot fallback.- Recognizes RFC-style `id` and `title` fields for catalog identity and labels so live ClawHub entries are not dropped before the later install-mapping slice.`n- Normalizes `public-clawhub` candidate `sha256:<hex>` integrity into SRI for the ClawHub source-profile install choice, while npm source profiles only preserve npm SRI-shaped integrity.## Deliberately Not Included- Feed/source profile persistence in user config files.- Secret-reference resolution or authenticated feed/source access.- Signed feed envelopes or trust-key configuration.- Scheduled refresh, `maxStale`, or stale-catalog status reporting.- Changing plugin install execution beyond resolving catalog metadata for configured source profiles.- Search, install UX, skills, or telemetry.## Validation- `pnpm exec oxfmt src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`- `pnpm exec oxlint --deny-warnings src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`- `git diff --check`- `CI=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts` (38 tests)- `pnpm tsgo:core`## Real Behavior ProofBehavior or issue addressed:This PR validates hosted marketplace install source refs against local source profiles and accepts the live ClawHub plugin feed format now served as `schemaVersion: 2`.Real environment tested:WSL Ubuntu 24.04, `/root/src/openclaw-feeds-hosted-catalog-prep`, PR5 and cumulative hosted marketplace feed stack through PR8 at `13cea8e00e7`, built source tree with `pnpm build`, isolated OpenClaw state via `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-feeds-proof.rKXnUX`, and the live ClawHub feed endpoint `https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins`.Exact steps or command run after this patch:1. On PR5: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts --reporter=dot`2. On PR8 after rebasing PR6-PR8: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.marketplace-refresh.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.marketplace-entries.test.ts src/plugins/marketplace.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.marketplaces.test.ts --reporter=dot`3. `curl -sS -D - https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins -o /tmp/clawhub-plugins.json`4. `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-feeds-proof.rKXnUX node openclaw.mjs plugins marketplace entries --feed-url https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins`Evidence after fix:PR5 focused catalog tests passed: `1 passed / 38 tests`. The focused source-profile tests cover ClawHub `sha256:<hex>` normalization for `public-clawhub` and prove npm source profiles do not treat that format as npm `expectedIntegrity`. Full stack focused tests passed across 3 Vitest shards: `1 passed / 8 tests`, `2 passed / 7 tests`, and `2 passed / 70 tests`. Live curl showed `HTTP/1.1 200 OK`, `X-Content-Sha256: 99b2608a539fe54d0d0ded2182574b62b8b772bd2011d9eb497147be23ff2c2e`, `Last-Modified: Wed, 24 Jun 2026 13:06:20 GMT`, and a body beginning with `{ "schemaVersion": 2, "id": "clawhub-official" ... }`. The built CLI returned `Source: hosted`, `Entries: 59`, `Feed: clawhub-official sequence 9`, and live ClawHub plugin entries.Observed result after fix:The live ClawHub v2 feed is accepted, entries with `public-clawhub` source refs resolve to `clawhub:` default install choices plus npm fallback metadata, ClawHub candidate SHA-256 integrity is normalized under the ClawHub source-profile path, and unsupported future schema versions still stay out of legacy parsing in tests.What was not tested:No live install was run. This PR validates and lists feed metadata only; install wiring remains a later slice.

target.title: perf: avoid event-loop stall during embedded_run bootstrap-context
target.body:
## Summary

Fix event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context that block the event loop for 14-22 seconds, causing message loss.

### Root causes addressed

1. **Synchronous file I/O** — `readFileSync`/`closeSync` in workspace file loading blocks the event loop while reading root bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.)
2. **Unbounded blocking glob walks** — `fs.glob` for extra bootstrap files traverses the entire workspace tree synchronously inside Node, including `.git`/`node_modules` directories, without yielding to the event loop
3. **Uninterrupted hook chains** — `triggerInternalHook` executes all handlers back-to-back without yielding

### Changes

**`src/agents/workspace.ts`**
- Replace sync `readFileSync`/`closeSync` with callback-based async `readFdToString`/`closeFdAsync`
- Parallelize root bootstrap file reads via `Promise.all` (previously serial `for` loop)
- Replace `fs.glob` with an async traversal walker that yields via `setImmediate` every 256 entries, so a sparse pattern like `**/AGENTS.md` over a large workspace never blocks the event loop. The walker performs no directory pruning — it walks the full tree and returns every file matching the configured patterns, with no traversal cutoff and no artificial match cap; the downstream bootstrap character budget owns content limiting.
- Preserve `fs.glob` no-match parity: a configured glob that matches nothing resolves to an empty set (not the literal pattern), so it no longer emits a phantom missing-file diagnostic; the literal `[pattern]` result is reserved for the no-glob-support fallback only.
- Suppress `closeFdAsync` rejections to avoid masking read errors on NFS/FUSE

**`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`** / **`src/hooks/internal-hook-dispatch.ts`**
- Keep the public `triggerInternalHook(event)` signature (re-exported via `plugin-sdk/hook-runtime`) unchanged and matching `main` — no new public parameter or exported options type.
- Move the bootstrap-only handler scheduling (`yieldBetweenHandlers` with `setImmediate` yield between handlers) and per-handler timing/slow-handler (>500ms) warning into an internal `internal-hook-dispatch` module that is not on the plugin SDK surface.

**`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`**
- Instrument bootstrap-context substages (`workspace-file-load`, `hook-overrides`) with `performance.now` timing
- Log substage durations when total bootstrap-context exceeds 2 seconds
- Enable yielding between handlers for `agent:bootstrap` hook triggers (via the internal dispatch entrypoint)

**`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`** / **`src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.ts`**
- Keep the public `resolveBootstrapFilesForRun` params (re-exported via `plugin-sdk/agent-harness-runtime`) callback-free and matching `main`; route embedded-runner substage timing through an internal `resolveBootstrapFilesForRunWithTiming` variant kept off the SDK surface.
- Support the new async workspace file loading interface.

### Tests

- `workspace.test.ts` — 3 new tests for async fd read (happy path, cache hit, fd close on failure)
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — every matching file returned without an artificial cap (no directory pruning), and a regression test asserting a late-appearing match in a large tree is still loaded (no traversal cutoff)
- `handler.test.ts` — no truncation warning is emitted for large glob scans
- `internal-hooks.test.ts` — yield-between-handlers ordering, handler timing reporting, and slow handler warning exercised through the internal dispatch entrypoint
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — a no-match glob resolves to an empty set with no missing-file diagnostic (fs.glob parity)

The public plugin SDK surface is unchanged: `node scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs` exits 0 with no new package-exported entrypoints. All existing tests pass without modification.

### Performance impact

- Root bootstrap file loading: serial sync → parallel async (estimated 3-5x speedup for 8 files)
- Glob walks: synchronous/blocking → async with periodic `setImmediate` yields (prevents multi-second event-loop stalls on large workspaces). The walk completes fully; all configured matches are kept, and the existing bootstrap character-budget truncation handles content limiting.
- Hook chains: uninterrupted → yielded (prevents monopolizing the event loop during bootstrap)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context loading caused by synchronous I/O and a blocking `fs.glob` walk over large workspaces, which blocks the loop and drops messages. This change makes the glob walk async/yielding so the loop stays responsive, while preserving every configured `bootstrap-extra-files` glob match (the earlier iteration of this branch added a hard 20,000-entry traversal cutoff that silently dropped late matches — that cutoff is removed here).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout on macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, run via `tsx` against the real exported `loadExtraBootstrapFiles` from `src/agents/workspace.ts`. Before/after captured from two git commits on the PR branch: before = `5849e709f4` (walker with the 20,000-entry cutoff), after = `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed).
- **Real environment scenario**: A workspace of ~26,100 filesystem entries (a 3-tier tree, 30×30×28 filler files) with a single configured-glob target `AGENTS.md` planted in the subtree the depth-first walker drains last, so the target is only reached after more than 20,000 visited entries. A 5ms repeating timer runs during the load as an event-loop liveness probe (steady ticks ⇒ the loop never stalled).
- **Failing proof before fix**: commit `5849e709f4` (cutoff present):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 82.9ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 16 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 0
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: false
  RESULT: late configured glob match was DROPPED (pre-fix behavior)
  ```
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  # against the patched checkout (commit 5ce395d645) a script that builds the
  # ~26,100-entry workspace, starts a 5ms liveness timer, then calls the real
  # loadExtraBootstrapFiles(root, ["**/AGENTS.md"]) and checks the deep match:
  npx tsx proof-live.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output from `node`/`tsx` against commit `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 99.4ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 20 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 1
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: true
  RESULT: configured glob match beyond old cutoff is PRESERVED; event loop stayed live
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the cutoff removed, the configured `**/AGENTS.md` match located past the old 20,000-entry frontier is now loaded (1 file returned vs 0 before in the terminal output above), so existing broad `bootstrap-extra-files` configs no longer silently lose AGENTS.md/TOOLS.md context. The liveness timer kept ticking throughout the async walk (20 ticks during the ~99ms load), confirming the periodic `setImmediate` yields — not the cutoff — are what keep the event loop responsive.
- **What was not tested**: No full live `embedded_run` with active channel connections was driven end-to-end; this proof exercises the real glob-resolution code path and event-loop liveness directly rather than through a connected channel. The focused regression coverage lives in `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` and `handler.test.ts`.

Closes #87509

target.title: fix(feishu): pace outbound sends per target
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #14206.

Feishu outbound sends currently retry after the API returns rate-limit responses, but configured high-volume conversations can still burst several `im.message.create` / `im.message.reply` calls to the same Feishu target at once. That makes the first user-visible symptom a Feishu rate-limit error instead of a paced send queue.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds an opt-in `channels.feishu.sendRateLimit.minIntervalMs` setting, with the same setting available under `channels.feishu.accounts.<id>.sendRateLimit.minIntervalMs` for per-account overrides. When configured, Feishu text, card, image, and file message sends are paced by account + receive id type + receive id. Different Feishu targets still send independently, and the default interval remains `0` so existing installs keep their current behavior unless they opt in.

## User Impact

Operators can set a small per-target send interval to avoid Feishu per-chat send bursts while keeping cross-chat throughput. Existing retry handling for Feishu 230020 / 11232 / 429 responses remains in place for server-side limits that still occur.

## Real Behavior Proof

Exact head: `0ab71706c99ee57338dcff1be74e06eb61ee2142`.

The after-fix pacing behavior is verified at the Feishu send-runtime seam with fake timers and mocked Feishu SDK calls. The proof covers both normal message sends and initial streaming-card sends, using the exact limiter key shape that production uses: account id + receive id type + receive id.

Same-target configured pacing proof:

```text
config: sendRateLimit.minIntervalMs = 50
send #1: to oc_paced, text first
send #2: to oc_paced, text second
send #3: to oc_paced, text third
at +0ms: Feishu message.create called 1 time
at +49ms: Feishu message.create still called 1 time
at +50ms: Feishu message.create called 2 times
after final interval: Feishu message.create called 3 times
observed: callTimes[1] - callTimes[0] >= 50
observed: callTimes[2] - callTimes[1] >= 50
```

Distinct-target independence proof:

```text
config: sendRateLimit.minIntervalMs = 50
send #1: to oc_paced_a
send #2: to oc_paced_b
at +0ms: both Feishu message.create calls completed
observed receive ids: oc_paced_a, oc_paced_b
observed: calls[1].at - calls[0].at == 0
```

Streaming-card initial send proof:

```text
config: sendRateLimit.minIntervalMs = 50
reply path: two initial streaming-card reply starts for the same target
  at +0ms: Feishu message.reply called 1 time
  at +49ms: Feishu message.reply still called 1 time
  at +50ms: Feishu message.reply called 2 times
  observed: sendTimes[1] - sendTimes[0] >= 50
create path: two initial streaming-card create starts for the same target
  at +0ms: Feishu message.create called 1 time
  at +49ms: Feishu message.create still called 1 time
  at +50ms: Feishu message.create called 2 times
  observed: sendTimes[1] - sendTimes[0] >= 50
```

GitHub exact-head proof/check evidence:

- `Real behavior proof` passed on this PR head: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28164570020/job/83413199211
- Full CI passed on this PR head: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28164195535
- The relevant exact-head CI checks include `check-test-types`, `check-prod-types`, `check-lint`, `check-docs`, `check-guards`, `checks-node-compact-*`, `QA Smoke CI`, and `ci-timings-summary`, all passing.

Live Feishu API proof was not run in this local environment because no Feishu app credentials are available here. The included proof exercises the runtime scheduling and SDK request seam without sending network traffic.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/send.concurrent.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send-target.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.retry.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/outbound.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts`
- `oxfmt --check extensions/feishu/src/send-rate-limit.ts extensions/feishu/src/send-target.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.ts extensions/feishu/src/media.ts extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.ts extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.concurrent.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts extensions/feishu/openclaw.plugin.json`
- `git diff --check`

Focused regression coverage includes:

- same-target text/card sends are serialized by the configured interval;
- distinct Feishu targets remain independent and are not serialized by each other's queue;
- media sends use the same per-target limiter;
- initial streaming-card reply and create sends use the limiter;
- invalid negative `sendRateLimit.minIntervalMs` values are rejected by schema;
- default config remains unpaced because the interval resolves to `0`.

target.title: fix(agents): isolated cron busts prompt prefix cache via per-run session id
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #96677 reports that since 2026.6.9 (still in 2026.6.10) every isolated cron run (`sessionTarget: isolated`) re-pays full **uncached** model input. The system prompt's `Runtime:` line embeds a per-run UUID inside the `session=` field (`session=agent:<agent>:cron:<jobid>:run:<RUN_UUID>`). `<RUN_UUID>` changes every run, so the byte-exact cached prefix is no longer stable and everything after the `Runtime:` line — notably the ~8k-token tool catalog — misses prompt prefix caching and is re-billed on every call. The reporter measured `input_tokens` jumping from ~200 (cached) to ~11,500 (uncached) per call on two independent metered providers; for multi-iteration cron agents this compounds to a ~10x cost increase. 2026.6.8 was unaffected. This re-introduces the cache-busting class previously fixed in #43148.
- **Root Cause**: `buildRuntimeLine` (`src/agents/system-prompt.ts`) renders `session=<sessionKey>` and `sessionId=<sessionId>` into the `Runtime:` line. #92453 (shipped 2026.6.8) added those fields intending *stable* identity; #91685 (2026.6.9) then made isolated cron session keys run-scoped (`agentSessionKey:run:<runSessionId>` in `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`) with a fresh session per run (`forceNew`), so the same volatile per-run UUID now flows into **both** the `session=` suffix and the `sessionId=` field (one shared value). The `Runtime:` line sits after the `OPENCLAW_CACHE_BOUNDARY` marker, so providers that emit an explicit cache breakpoint keep it in the uncached suffix and were never affected. But providers that strip the boundary and rely on automatic literal-prefix caching (e.g. `openai-completions` when the compat profile carries no `cache_control`; `openai-transport-stream` strips the marker) send the whole prompt as the cached byte stream, so the volatile line breaks the byte prefix and the tool definitions serialized after it miss cache every run. Stripping only `session=` would leave `sessionId=` equally volatile, so both are addressed.
- **Fix**: Render cache-stable identity. A new `parseCronRunScopeSuffix` helper in `src/sessions/session-key-utils.ts` splits the terminal `:run:<id>` scope off a cron key, yielding the stable base key. `buildRuntimeLine` renders that base key for `session=` and drops the `sessionId=` field when it equals the per-run id the scope duplicates. The base key `agent:<agent>:cron:<jobid>` is exactly the cache-stable form rendered before 2026.6.9, so old prompt bytes are preserved. Byte-stabilizing the rendered identity is the right lever here: the affected providers strip the cache boundary, so moving the field below it would not help them.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/sessions/session-key-utils.ts` — add `parseCronRunScopeSuffix` (splits the terminal `:run:<id>` scope from cron keys only, returning the cache-stable base key and the run id).
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.ts` — `buildRuntimeLine` renders the base key for `session=` and omits the per-run `sessionId=`.
  - `src/routing/session-key.test.ts` — unit tests for `parseCronRunScopeSuffix`.
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` — runtime-line cache-stability behavior tests.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - The run-scoped delivery/queue routing key from #91685 is untouched; only the cached prompt rendering changes.
  - Non-run-scoped keys (normal channels, subagents, ACP, and sessions whose id is not the run id) render byte-for-byte as before.
  - Providers that emit an explicit cache breakpoint already keep the `Runtime:` line in the uncached suffix, so this is a no-op for them; it restores cache only for the automatic-literal-prefix-caching providers the issue measured.
  - `parseCronRunScopeSuffix` is gated to the isolated-cron key shape (`agent:<id>:cron:<job>:run:<runId>`); the run scope is only ever appended to cron keys, so a `:run:` segment embedded in any other session key (e.g. a channel-owned conversation id) is returned unchanged and never truncated.
  - Config surface unchanged (no schema, defaults, doctor migrations, or `docs/reference/config`).
  - Plugin surface unchanged (no plugin SDK, manifest, `extensions/*` api/runtime-api, registry/loader).
  - Gateway protocol unchanged.

### Reproduction

1. Create an isolated cron job (`sessionTarget: isolated`) on a metered provider that reports `input_tokens`.
2. Run it twice and capture the system prompt's `Runtime:` line and `input_tokens`/call for each run.
3. Compare the cached prefix bytes and token counts between the two runs.
4. **Before this PR**: the `Runtime:` line reads `session=agent:<agent>:cron:<jobid>:run:<UUID>` with a new UUID each run (and `sessionId=<UUID>` carries the same value), so the two runs differ byte-for-byte, the tool catalog misses cache, and `input_tokens` jumps ~50x (~200 → ~11,500).
5. **After this PR**: the line reads `session=agent:<agent>:cron:<jobid>` with no `:run:` token and no volatile `sessionId=`, identical across runs, so the cached prefix is reused and `input_tokens` returns to the cached baseline.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#96677)**: the cached `Runtime:` line no longer carries the volatile per-run id; two isolated-cron runs of the same job render byte-identical lines, so prefix caching for the tool catalog after the line is preserved.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 24 — Vitest against the production `buildRuntimeLine` and `parseCronRunScopeSuffix`)**: the runtime-line renderer and the session-key helper are exercised directly, with no harness shims.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts src/routing/session-key.test.ts`; `pnpm tsgo`; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` and `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the four changed files.

**Evidence after fix (Vitest output for the touched test files)**:

```text
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  168 passed (168)
```

**Observed result after fix**: for a run-scoped key `agent:work:cron:nightly-job:run:<UUID>` the line renders `session=agent:work:cron:nightly-job` with no `:run:` token and no `sessionId=`; two distinct run UUIDs produce identical lines. A non-run-scoped key (`agent:main:main`) keeps both `session=` and its distinct `sessionId=`. `pnpm tsgo` exits 0; oxfmt/oxlint report the changed files clean.

**What was not tested**: the end-to-end metered-provider token measurement on a live isolated-cron deployment; the fix is proven deterministically at the render boundary instead.

**Repro confirmation**: the new cache-stability test fails on `main` (the run UUID leaks into `session=`/`sessionId=`, so the two runs render different lines) and passes with this change.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: a stable session id that is not a run id gets dropped from the prompt. **Mitigation**: `sessionId=` is dropped only when it equals the parsed `:run:` id; non-run-scoped keys have an undefined run id and always keep their session id — covered by a test.
- **Risk**: a key containing `:run:` as data is mis-parsed. **Mitigation**: only keys matching the cron-run shape (`agent:<id>:cron:<job>:run:<runId>`) are stripped; any other key — including one that embeds `:run:` elsewhere — is returned unchanged — covered by tests.
- **Risk**: prompt identity regression for agents. **Mitigation**: `session=` still carries the stable base key (the pre-2026.6.9 form), so agents keep session identity without the volatile per-run token.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #96677

target.title: fix(heartbeat): remove static iteration cap in seekNextActivePhaseDueMs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where agents with sub-minute heartbeat intervals and active
hours would permanently stop running heartbeats when the current time fell
outside the active window — the static 10,080 iteration cap was calibrated
for 1-minute intervals and exhausted too early for shorter intervals.

## Why This Change Was Made

**Unified batched seek at >= 30 s step** with backward walk on
inactive->active transitions:

\`\`\`ts
const MIN_SEEK_STEP_MS = 30_000;
const multiplier = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(MIN_SEEK_STEP_MS / intervalMs));
const batchStepMs = intervalMs * multiplier;
// … backward walk on prevWasActive===false -> active transition
\`\`\`

| intervalMs | multiplier | batchStepMs | max iterations | narrow windows |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 60 s+ | 1 | ≤ 60 s | ≤ 10,080 | ✅ per-candidate |
| 30 s | 1 | 30 s | 20,160 | ✅ per-candidate |
| 59 s | 1 | 59 s | 10,251 | ✅ per-candidate |
| 1 s | 30 | 30 s | 20,160 | ✅ 30 s < min window |
| 500 ms | 60 | 30 s | 20,160 | ✅ backward walk |
| 1 ms | 30,000 | 30 s | 20,160 | ✅ backward walk |

Max **20,160** production \`isWithinActiveHours\` predicate calls
regardless of intervalMs (~20–200 ms for Intl.DateTimeFormat per call).

## User Impact

Users with sub-minute heartbeat intervals and active hours can correctly
seek into the next active window. No impact on minute-or-larger intervals
(multiplier = 1).

## Evidence

**Unit tests (19/19 pass):** 14 existing + 5 new
- 30s / 09:00-17:00: finds 09:00 next day, phase-aligned
- **59s / 1min narrow window (09:00-09:01)**: finds 09:00:43 (977 steps)
- 500ms, startMs active: returns directly (no backward walk)
- 500ms, inactive->active transition: backward walk finds 09:00:00
- 1ms / never-active: bounded at 20,160 iter, sub-50ms

**Real behavior proof (\`node --import tsx\`, 6 scenarios):**
\`\`\`
=== seekNextActivePhaseDueMs v6 (30s unified batch, <= 20,160 calls) ===

1. 30s, 09:00-17:00, start 17:00 -> 09:00:00 next day (active: true)
2. 59s, 09:00-09:01 window, start 17:00 -> 09:00:43 (steps: 977)
3. 7s, always-active, start 12:00 -> 12:00 (=startMs)
4. 1s, never-active, start 12:00 -> 12:00 (elapsed: 0.6ms)
5. computeNextHeartbeatPhaseDueMs (30s), now 10:00 -> 10:00:30
6. Full-path: 30s, 20:00->09-17 active -> 09:00:15 next day (active: true)
\`\`\`

target.title: fix(reply): suppress post-tool-send meta commentary as duplicate reply
target.body:
Closes #96681

## What Problem This Solves

After an agent calls the `message` tool (`action=send`) to deliver its main reply in a turn, models often append a short trailing meta-acknowledgement to their final reply — patterns like `已发 #22141`, `Sent above`, `核心回答如下`, `OK`, `Roger`, `Got it`, `主回复已发 (#xxx)`, `已发, 不再追加总结`, `Sent. Replied in thread.`, `Done.`, `Okay`, `OK, that's the fix.`

These acks carry no information beyond the message-tool send that already went out, but OpenClaw currently delivers them as a **second** visible message in the channel — a well-known duplicate-reply failure mode (F30 / F31).

The existing `filterMessagingToolDuplicates` only catches full-content duplicates via substring match with a 10-char minimum, so short meta-acks (e.g. `已发 #22141`, 9 chars) escape the floor and reach the channel as a second visible message. The current dedupe is correct for "is this a rephrasing of something I already sent" but does not handle "is this an agent ack of its own tool call".

## Fix

This patch adds a complementary filter — `filterMessagingToolMetaCommentary` — that recognises post-tool-send meta commentary by pattern (Chinese + English) and suppresses it from the final reply payload. Wired into both `agent-runner-payloads.ts` and `followup-delivery.ts` **after** the existing content-dedupe filter, so the two compose without changing each other's behaviour.

The new filter is intentionally conservative:

- **Only fires when a message-tool send has already happened this turn** (`sentTexts.length > 0`). Agents with no message-tool sends are unaffected and short acks still pass through normally.
- **Length-capped at 200 chars** and only matches anchored ack patterns.
- **Substantial trailing content after the ack (> 20 chars) is treated as a real reply** — e.g. `已发. Now let me explain the architecture ...` is **NOT** suppressed.
- **Media payloads are always preserved**, even if the caption is meta-ack.

### Patterns matched

| Group | Examples |
| --- | --- |
| Chinese standalone acks | `已发`, `已发送`, `已发 #22141`, `已发, 不再追加`, `主回复已发`, `主回复已发 (#xxx)`, `核心回答如下`, `总结如下`, `已发完成` |
| English standalone acks | `Sent`, `Sent above`, `Sent #22141`, `Sent.`, `Sent. Replied in thread.`, `Done.`, `Done`, `OK.`, `OK, that's the fix.`, `Roger`, `Roger that.`, `Got it`, `Got it.`, `Okay`, `Gotcha` |
| Suppressed prefix acks | `Replied`, `Replied above`, `Replied in thread`, `See above`, `As above` |
| CJK with optional trailing ref | `已发 #N`, `已发送 (#N)`, `主回复已发 (#N)` |

### Why a second filter, not extending the first

The existing content dedupe is correct for its purpose (substring match against `sentTexts`, 10-char floor). Extending it to 0-char would break legitimate short replies. A separate axis — "is this an ack of my own tool call" — only fires when there is a `message` tool send in the same turn, and only against a pattern list. The two filters compose: an ack that also matches the sent text still gets caught by the content filter; an ack that doesn't (e.g. `OK`, `Sent above` after a long body) gets caught by the new one.

## Evidence

### Test coverage (added in `reply-payloads.test.ts`)

New test cases for `filterMessagingToolMetaCommentary`:
- Suppresses `已发`, `已发 #22141`, `已发, 不再追加总结` after a `message` send
- Suppresses `Sent above`, `Sent.`, `Sent #22141`, `OK.`, `Roger`, `Got it`, `Done.` after a `message` send
- **Preserves** `OK.` when there is no prior message-tool send (ack is the entire reply)
- **Preserves** `已发. Now let me explain the architecture ...` (substantial trailing content)
- **Preserves** `Roger the new release looks good` (pattern doesn't anchor)
- **Preserves** media payloads (any caption containing a meta-ack is kept if the payload has media)
- Composition: ack after a long body that does not match sent text — only the meta filter catches it
- Composition: ack that also matches a substring of sent text — content filter catches it first, meta filter has nothing to do

### CI status on this PR (run 28152284825)

- ✅ `check-lint`
- ✅ `check-test-types`, `check-prod-types`
- ✅ `check-shrinkwrap`, `check-dependencies`, `check-guards`
- ✅ `check-session-accessor-boundary`, `check-session-transcript-reader-boundary`
- ✅ `check-additional-boundaries-a/bcd`, `check-additional-extension-{channels,bundled,package-boundary}`
- ✅ `check-additional-runtime-topology-architecture`
- ✅ `checks-fast-bundled-protocol`, `checks-fast-bun-launcher`
- ✅ `checks-fast-contracts-plugins-a/b`, `checks-fast-contracts-channels-a/b`
- ✅ `checks-node-compact-small-{1..10,whole-1,2,3}`, `checks-node-compact-large-{1,2,3,whole-1}`
- ✅ `build-artifacts`, `QA Smoke CI`, `preflight`, `security-fast`
- ✅ CodeQL `Security High` (core-auth-secrets, channel-runtime-boundary, network-ssrf-boundary, mcp-process-tool-boundary, plugin-trust-boundary, actions)
- ✅ OpenGrep (precise diff scan + OSS)
- ✅ Dependency Guard, Security Sensitive Guard, Workflow Sanity
- ✅ ClawSweeper Dispatch, Labeler
- ✅ `auto-response` (ClawSweeper)
- ❌ `Real behavior proof` — see "PR body format check" below

### PR body format check failure

The `Real behavior proof` check (`scripts/github/real-behavior-proof-check.mjs`) is a **PR body format gate** for external contributors, not a behavioral test. On this PR it failed with:

> External PRs must include authored What Problem This Solves and Evidence sections.

The original PR body had `## Problem` and `## Fix` headings. This updated body renames/expands those into the required `## What Problem This Solves` and `## Evidence` sections above. No code change. Re-running the check is expected to flip to green.

### Files changed (8 files, +225 / −3)

- `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-payloads-dedupe.ts` — add `filterMessagingToolMetaCommentary` + pattern tables
- `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-payloads-dedupe.runtime.ts` — runtime helpers (trim, length cap, anchor matcher)
- `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-payloads.ts` — wire new filter into the dedupe pipeline
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.ts` — call the new filter after the existing content-dedupe
- `src/auto-reply/reply/followup-delivery.ts` — same wiring for the followup path
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/messaging-dedupe.ts` — re-export the new filter for shared use
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.ts` — surface the filter in the embedded helper API
- `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-payloads.test.ts` — test cases listed above

### Live behavior

Local repro of the original issue, after applying this patch, on a Telegram-routed reply that includes a trailing `已发 #22141`:
- Before: 2 visible messages (main reply + `已发 #22141`)
- After: 1 visible message (main reply only; ack suppressed)

Same result on Discord and Signal (both go through `deliverOutboundPayloads`).

target.title: test(ui-e2e): run Chromium capability probe headless with a timeout
target.body:
## Problem

`canRunPlaywrightChromium()` in `ui/src/test-helpers/control-ui-e2e.ts` gates the
ui-e2e suites by shelling out to `chrome.exe --version` synchronously (`spawnSync`),
and the helper runs at test-module load time.

On **Chrome for Testing 148 (Playwright `chromium-1223`) on Windows**, `chrome.exe
--version` does **not** print-and-exit — it launches a full **headed** browser that
never returns. Because the probe is synchronous, the entire `vitest` ui-e2e run
**deadlocks at the `RUN` header before any test executes**, and a visible "New Tab -
Google Chrome for Testing" window appears. Playwright revision **1208**'s `--version`
exits cleanly, so this is a CFT-148 regression on Windows.

The tests themselves are not the problem: driving `chromium.launch({ headless: true })`
with the matching binary directly is fully windowless. Only the capability probe hangs.

## Fix

Run the probe **headless with a hard timeout**:

- a clean `exit 0` still confirms the binary runs — unchanged fast path on Linux/CI,
  where `--version` exits immediately;
- a timeout falls back to the on-disk existence check (the binary is present;
  Playwright's own `chromium.launch()` surfaces any genuine launch failure) instead of
  blocking forever or popping a window.

## Verification

- **Before:** a `ui/src/ui/e2e/*.e2e.test.ts` spec on Windows (chromium-1223) hangs at
  `RUN`; a headed "Chrome for Testing" window is left open (reproduced standalone with
  `chrome.exe --version`).
- **After:** the spec runs to completion and stays **windowless** — 0 headed windows
  observed across the run, no lingering Chrome-for-Testing processes.
- No behavior change on Linux/CI, where `--version` already exits 0 immediately.

Based on `dd9706e90264e272ce7f155e00fa3cc83ee69b7a`; happy to rebase onto current `main`.

target.title: Harden embedded memory flush isolation
target.body:
## Summary

This PR hardens embedded pre-attempt memory flush behavior and adds provider compatibility support for Responses system prompt placement.

Memory lifecycle focus:

- This stage targets the **memory write/update** and **pre-compaction flush** boundary.
- The synthetic memory flush route now avoids carrying pending-turn user content/media.
- The flush keeps the routing/session/policy metadata needed for the memory operation, while the actual model input is driven by the memory flush plan prompt.
- This helps separate memory lifecycle work from live conversation context, especially around which context should be isolated vs shared.

Implementation changes:

- Strips pending-turn content/media from embedded synthetic flush routes:
  - `prompt`
  - `transcriptPrompt`
  - inbound event/audio/context fields
  - `images`
  - `imageOrder`
- Keeps the pending prompt available separately only for flush threshold estimation.
- Adds regression coverage with fake sensitive pending-turn content to ensure it does not ride along into the flush route.
- Adds `systemPromptMode` compatibility for OpenAI Responses-style providers:
  - default `message`
  - top-level `instructions`
  - first `user` message for providers that reject system/developer fields
- Extends config/schema/types coverage for the new compat field.

## Validation

Focused tests passed:

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/memory-flush.test.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/memory-flush.embedded-preattempt.test.ts`
- `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts -t "moves Responses system prompts into a first user message when compat requires user mode"`
- `src/llm/providers/openai-compatible-auth.test.ts -t "Responses system prompts"`
- `git diff --check`

## Known caveat

A full run of the large `openai-transport-stream.test.ts` file still shows unrelated existing failures in ModelStudio/DeepSeek/DashScope compatibility sections. The focused tests covering this PR's changed transport behavior pass.

## Follow-up

Next lifecycle stage after this PR: validate memory retrieval when a user starts a new conversation, including source selection, session boundaries, private/group isolation, and how retrieved memory relates to conversation context.

target.title: feat: show live session activity for running tasks
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

  Resolves a problem where the control UI could not reliably know which background work was active for a session, making it hard to show loading state, running task details, or polling-style progress for exec calls, subagents, and other session-scoped work.

  ## Why This Change Was Made

  This adds a live session activity surface over the existing gateway WebSocket protocol instead of introducing a separate REST endpoint, so the UI can subscribe to activity changes using the same session state channel. The snapshot aggregates active task-registry entries and live tool calls, includes descendant sessions by default, and exposes metadata-only plugin hooks for other integrations to build on without leaking tool arguments or results.

  ## User Impact

  Users can now see when a session has active background work, including exec/tool activity and task activity from subagents or related session work. The control UI can show a loading controller and running task list from the new session activity stream, while plugin authors get hook events for task and tool lifecycle changes.

  ## Evidence

  OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 PATH=/Users/gauravprasad/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.2/bin:$PATH corepack pnpm test src/gateway/session-activity.test.ts src/gateway/session-activity.snapshot.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/session-activity.test.ts src/tasks/task-registry.store.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/session-activity.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts

target.title: fix(agents): keep lightweight cron prompts out of no-op compaction
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96658.

Lightweight isolated cron runs on very small context windows can reserve too much of the model window for response headroom before the first prompt is sent. In the reported 4,096-token model case, current `main` leaves only 2,048 prompt tokens for a 3,544-token lightweight cron prompt, so the precheck routes to `compact_only` even though lightweight cron has no bootstrap history to compact.

## Why This Change Was Made

This keeps the shared small-context prompt floor conservative: `MIN_PROMPT_BUDGET_RATIO` remains `0.5` for full-context callers, settings reserve capping, CLI compaction, mid-turn guard paths, and lightweight shared-history runs such as non-isolated heartbeats.

The higher 87.5% prompt share is now scoped to history-free lightweight pre-prompt admission only. The precheck also subtracts estimated tool-schema pressure before accepting `fits`, using the provider-visible tool surface from both runtime tools and client-supplied tool definitions.

`toolSchemaTokens` is diagnostic-only and optional on `PreemptiveCompactionDecision`, preserving the public `openclaw/plugin-sdk/agent-harness-runtime` type shape for existing SDK consumers.

## User Impact

Lightweight cron jobs on small-context fallback models can proceed when the rendered prompt plus tool schema surface fits the model window, instead of failing through repeated compact-only recovery with nothing useful to compact.

Tool-heavy small-context runs and shared-history lightweight runs remain conservative, and non-lightweight/full-context paths keep the previous shared reserve behavior.

## Evidence

Earlier real lightweight cron behavior proof on this PR branch:

```text
Setup: source Gateway from this PR branch, temp HOME and OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR, local mock OpenAI Responses server.
Config: openai/gpt-5.5 contextWindow=4096 contextTokens=4096, agents.defaults.compaction.reserveTokens=20000.
Action: cron.add with sessionTarget=isolated, payload.kind=agentTurn, payload.lightContext=true, then cron.run mode=force.
Result: cron finished status=ok; mock provider received 2 POST /v1/responses requests.
```

Gateway debug proof from that run:

```text
[context-overflow-precheck] pre-prompt check ... provider=openai/gpt-5.5 route=fits estimatedPromptTokens=3318 pressureSource=llm_boundary_normalized_prompt promptBudgetBeforeReserve=3584 overflowTokens=0 toolResultReducibleChars=0 reserveTokens=20000 effectiveReserveTokens=512 contextTokenBudget=4096 messages=0 unwindowedMessages=0 sessionFile=<tmp-state>/agents/main/sessions/<session>.jsonl
```

Current-head boundary proof for the reported 4,096-token / 20,000-reserve / 3,544-token case:

```text
history-free lightweight, no tools: route=fits, promptBudgetBeforeReserve=3584, toolSchemaTokens=0, effectiveReserveTokens=512
history-free lightweight, 570-token schema: route=compact_only, promptBudgetBeforeReserve=3014, overflowTokens=530
lightweight with shared history: route=compact_only, promptBudgetBeforeReserve=2048
full mode, no tools: route=compact_only, promptBudgetBeforeReserve=2048
```

Red test before the original fix:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.test.ts
AssertionError: expected 2048 to be greater than or equal to 3544
```

Focused validation on current head `26ea90c853a`:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.test.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts
# PASS 39 tests

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/agent-harness-runtime.test.ts
# PASS 16 tests

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
# PASS

node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts src/plugin-sdk/agent-harness-runtime.test.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts
# PASS

git diff --check
# PASS
```

Review comments addressed:

- Codex's original tool-schema budgeting concern: addressed by subtracting tool schema pressure before history-free lightweight `fits` admission.
- Codex's client-supplied tools follow-up: addressed by budgeting `effectiveTools` plus `clientToolDefs`, matching the provider-visible custom tool surface used for the session.
- Codex's heartbeat/shared-history follow-up: addressed by keeping lightweight runs with any history on the shared 2,048-token floor for 4k models.
- ClawSweeper's SDK compatibility finding: addressed by making `toolSchemaTokens` optional and adding a plugin SDK type compatibility test that constructs the old decision shape.

Autoreview: attempted one local draft-PR review round, but the helper failed before review due local Codex config parsing (`service_tier = priority`; helper expected `fast` or `flex`). No review findings were produced.

Agent transcript: a matching local session was found, but no transcript is included because adding sanitized transcript logs requires explicit approval.

target.title: fix(codex): surface native subagent task updates
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where operators using channel-backed Codex multi-agent runs could not see native subagent lifecycle updates when a Codex native subagent was spawned. The mirrored task rows were hidden from delivery even though the parent run had a real channel target.

Fixes #87666.

## Why This Change Was Made

Native Codex subagent mirror rows now derive delivery from the parent requester origin. When the parent origin has both `channel` and `to`, the mirrored subagent task is created as `state_changes` / `pending` and retains `requesterOrigin` for direct delivery; when no delivery target is available, orphan/internal mirror rows keep the existing `silent` / `not_applicable` behavior.

The gateway and embedded Codex runner now pass the resolved delivery context into the harness task runtime scope used by the native subagent monitor. I also checked the sibling Codex source: app-server protocol emits the `thread/started` and `thread/status/changed` notifications consumed here, thread-spawn metadata carries the parent thread id and agent labels, and collab spawn/wait events map into `collabAgentToolCall` items that this mirror already observes.

## User Impact

Operators on channel-backed parent Codex runs can receive native subagent task state changes instead of having mirror tasks hidden by `silent` / `not_applicable`. Internal or orphan native subagent mirrors remain quiet.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-task-mirror.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.test.ts` (51 tests passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/agent-harness-task-runtime.test.ts src/tasks/task-executor-policy.test.ts src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts` (95 tests passed)
- `npm run tsgo:test:src`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt.CMD --check --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-task-mirror.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-task-mirror.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.test.ts src/agents/command/types.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/params.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Not run: live channel proof. This needs a configured channel/gateway environment and maintainer confirmation of the chosen delivery behavior.

`npm run check:changed` was attempted, but it stopped before code checks because `pnpm install` aborted in this non-TTY environment with `ERR_PNPM_ABORTED_REMOVE_MODULES_DIR_NO_TTY`.
